data.title: Old Deng didn't die, he just became a token.
data.description:
That night we had hot pot in Palo Alto. The beef had just been put into the pot when the wine was poured in. Alan Walker leaned back in his chair, looking at the group of entrepreneurs, investors, and engineers, and said calmly: "Stop asking what this era has done to Old Deng. Old Deng didn't die. He was just compressed, crushed, and abstracted by this era, ultimately becoming a token. In the past, they were people, seniors, bosses, experts, a whole set of experience and judgment; today, they've been broken down into smaller, lighter, cheaper, and easier-to-use units. In the past, they were called people; today, they're called tokens." A moment of silence fell over the table. Everyone knew this wasn't an emotion; it was reality. What has changed is no longer just tools, jobs, and industries; it's the very way people exist. The cruelest thing isn't being eliminated, but being dismantled first. Many people think the most terrifying thing in this era is unemployment. Actually, no. What's truly terrifying is that before you even leave the stage, you've already been dismantled. Your time, experience, expression, judgment, processes, and habits will all be broken down into smaller parts, extracted, reused, and transferred. In the past, people were valuable because many abilities could only reside within a single person. What this era demands is not a complete person, but rather the most readily available part of you. The first principle is simple: What can be expressed can be recorded. What can be recorded can be organized. What can be organized can be learned. What can be learned can eventually be tokenized. So, Old Deng didn't suddenly become worthless. Rather, their most valuable assets, for the first time, no longer needed to be fully attached to them. This is the cold reality: the world begins to realize that it can continue to use you without needing you as a complete person.

What Old Deng lost was not his position, but the premium of his "whole person"

Alan said something very important that night:

A person's life, in the end, will all be melted down into something more easily circulated

When people are young, they always feel that they should leave something behind in their lives.

Companies, works, methodologies, reputation, people they have mentored, judgments made after seeing through the world.

Today, many people will slowly realize that they have indeed left something behind, but the way they left it behind has changed.

What you've written will be dismantled. What you've said will be collected. The order in which you do things will be organized. The judgments, aesthetics, experience, and sense of proportion you've honed will eventually become more refined, fragmented, standardized, and easily circulated. They will continue to exist, but no longer as a whole. This is the most tragic part. A person works hard their whole life, always thinking they are slowly building a solid foundation for their life. But with the arrival of the new era, many weighty things have been reprocessed: **thinned out, broken down, compressed, numbered, and priced.** What remains in the end is not how many detours you took, how many nights you stayed up, or how many grievances you endured. What remains are the **easiest parts to absorb.** It's not that you've lived in vain. It's that much of what you've lived has ultimately rendered you unnecessary.

Old Deng didn't die, he just heard the sound of his own shattering earlier than others

In the end, Old Deng isn't a specific age group. Today's Old Deng is just everyone tomorrow.

You think you're still young, still fast, still able to keep up.

You think you're still young, still fast, still able to keep up.

data.title: NYSE explores introducing blockchain technology; Iran has responded to US negotiating conditions.
data.description:
Headline

▌NYSE: Exploring the Integration of Blockchain Technology into Existing Market Infrastructure

On March 27, Jon Herrick, Head of Product at the New York Stock Exchange (NYSE), stated that the NYSE is currently exploring integrating blockchain technology into its existing market infrastructure, rather than replacing traditional systems. The core objective is to achieve interoperability while preserving existing regulatory, clearing, and investor protection mechanisms. Herrick pointed out that the future development path is not a choice between traditional finance and blockchain, but rather a gradual integration. He emphasized that while promoting the application of tokenized assets, it is necessary to consider the mature advantages of the current market system. Currently, the NYSE is researching application scenarios including near real-time settlement and extended trading hours. Its parent company, Intercontinental Exchange (ICE), has also entered the crypto space, recently investing in OKX and planning to introduce crypto price data into derivatives while promoting tokenized asset-related businesses.

However, Herrick also stated that the existing centralized clearing system still has advantages in reducing transaction risks and is unlikely to be completely replaced in the short term. In the long term, the boundary between traditional assets and tokenized assets may gradually become blurred. Overall, the NYSE prefers a gradual introduction of blockchain technology rather than a radical restructuring of the financial system.

▌Iran has formally responded to the "15-point plan," asserting that the US's negotiating statement is a "third deception."

March 26th news, according to Iran's Tasnim News Agency, informed sources stated that Iran formally submitted its response to the "15-point proposal" to the US last night through intermediaries and is currently awaiting a US reply. Iran's response included the following core demands: ① The enemy's terrorist aggression must cease; ② Objective conditions must be established to ensure that war will not be repeated; ③ Compensation and restitution for war losses must be clearly defined and guaranteed; ④ The ceasefire must be implemented simultaneously on all fronts and in all resistance organizations involved in the fighting throughout the region.

... He further emphasized that Iran's exercise of sovereignty over the Strait of Hormuz is its natural and legitimate right, and a guarantee to ensure the other party fulfills its commitments, which must be recognized. The source pointed out that Iran has determined that the US's negotiating statements are a "third deception" scheme, with three true objectives: ① to deceive the international community by fabricating a peaceful posture; ② to depress global oil prices; and ③ to buy time for a ground invasion from southern Iran. (Golden Ten) Market Data As of press time, according to CoinGecko data: BTC price is $68,706.90, a 24-hour change of -3.5%; ETH price is $2,057.56, a 24-hour change of -5.0%; BNB price is $628.28, a 24-hour change of -2.9%; SOL price is $86.33, a 24-hour change of... rgb(255, 0, 0);">-5.8%；

DOGE price is $0.09187, 24-hour change -4.3%；

XRP price is $1.36, 24-hour change -4.0%；

TRX price is $0.3122, 24-hour change -1.3%；

WLFI price is $0.09806, 24-hour change -5.8%；

DOGE price is $0.09187, 24-hour change ...24-hour change 24-hour change

▌Trump Reiterates: Iran is in Talks with the US

On March 26, it was learned that US President Trump stated that Iran is in talks with the US. Trump said they will wait and see if a proper agreement can be reached and the Strait of Hormuz opened. Iran must reach an agreement, otherwise it will face continued and fierce attacks from the US.

... (CCTV News) Trump Postpones Strike on Iranian Power Plants for Another 10 Days On March 27, US President Trump posted on his social media platform "Real Social," stating, "At the request of the Iranian government, I hereby declare that I will postpone the strike on [Iran's] energy facilities for another 10 days, until 8 p.m. Eastern Time on April 6, 2026." Trump also stated, "Negotiations are ongoing, and despite false and contradictory statements from fake news media and others, the negotiations are progressing very well." (CCTV International News) Blockchain Applications ... ARK announced that it will use data from the prediction market platform Kalshi to guide investment decisions and hedge risks. ARK stated that it will use Kalshi in three ways: first, to provide "continuously updated expectations" to supplement fundamental and quantitative analysis; second, to obtain real-time market expectations through indicators such as trading volume; and third, for event-driven risk management, including hedging against specific outcomes affecting portfolios as well as macroeconomic and industry risks. Cathie Wood pointed out that introducing prediction markets into institutional investment processes is a natural extension of financial research innovation, helping to quantify uncertainty and improve decision-making quality. Nick Grous, Research Director at ARK, stated that prediction markets can more purely reflect risk expectations for key economic and corporate events.

▌Google: Developing a Post-Quantum Cryptography Migration Timeline, Aiming for Completion by 2029

On March 26, Google announced its 2029 timeline, planning to complete its post-quantum cryptography (PQC) migration to ensure data security in the quantum era.

Google stated last month that it had already called for building robust security defenses for the quantum era before future quantum computers can break existing encryption algorithms. This new timeline was developed to adapt to the migration needs of the post-quantum cryptography era, taking into account factors such as the progress of quantum computing hardware development, quantum error correction technology, and quantum factorization resource calculations. Quantum computers will pose a significant threat to current cryptographic standards, especially to encryption algorithms and digital signature technologies. Encryption algorithms already face threats such as "store-first, decrypt-later" attacks; while digital signatures face future threats, requiring a transition to post-quantum cryptography before the advent of quantum computers (CRQC) with actual cryptographic threats. Therefore, we have adjusted our threat response model, prioritizing the post-quantum cryptographic migration of identity authentication services—a crucial step in cybersecurity and digital signature upgrades. We recommend that other technology teams adopt the same strategy.

Cryptocurrency

▌Coinbase and Better Launch Crypto-Backed Mortgages, Supporting BTC and USDC as Collateral

Coinbase announced a partnership with US online mortgage platform Better to launch its first compliant crypto-backed mortgage product, allowing users to use Bitcoin or USDC from their Coinbase accounts as collateral to pay for a down payment on a home. The mortgage is issued and managed by Better and has the same legal protection as traditional compliant mortgages. The product is structured as a "dual loan model": one is a standard mortgage, and the other is a down payment loan secured by crypto assets. Both have the same interest rate and term. Users only need to pay one combined monthly payment. The collateral will be held in custodian in a Coinbase Prime account and returned after the loan is repaid. The mortgage terms are unaffected by BTC price fluctuations.

▌Fannie Mae to Accept Crypto Asset-Backed Mortgages for the First Time

On March 26, according to the Wall Street Journal, Fannie Mae, a "super giant" in the U.S. mortgage market, will accept mortgages secured by crypto assets for the first time. This marks the formal inclusion of cryptocurrencies into the institution's mortgage underwriting system, allowing borrowers to use digital assets to qualify for loans or as collateral. Advantages: Expands financing channels for crypto asset holders, avoids tax liquidation, and gains recognition for digital assets in the mainstream housing finance sector. Risks: Crypto asset volatility could lead to a surge in default rates, and Fannie Mae's government backing could expose taxpayers to potential risks.

... According to Arkham data, BlackRock transferred a total of 15,405 ETH, worth approximately $32.02 million, to a Coinbase Prime address through its Ethereum exchange-traded fund ETHA; and a total of 1,133.658 BTC, worth approximately $78.83 million, to a Coinbase Prime address through its Bitcoin exchange-traded fund IBIT. On March 26, Bitcoin mining company MARA announced a $1 billion convertible senior bond repurchase program. The repurchase of the 2030 and 2031 notes is expected to be completed on March 30 and 31, 2026, respectively, subject to customary closing conditions. The repurchase transaction is expected to generate approximately $88.1 million in cash reserves for the company. The company also announced today that it sold 15,133 bitcoins between March 4 and March 25, 2026, for a total transaction value of approximately $1.1 billion. The proceeds from the bitcoin sales are expected to be used for the note repurchase transaction, with the remainder used for general corporate purposes. **Strategy CEO: Approximately 40% of MSTR Shares are Held by Retail Investors, While Retail Investors Hold Up to 80% of STRC** On March 26th, Phong Le, CEO of Bitcoin treasury company Strategy ($MSTR), stated that approximately 40% of MicroStrategy (MSTR) shares are held by retail investors, while retail investors hold up to 80% of STRC shares. **Yi Lihua: The War Is Unlikely to End in the Short Term, But There Will Definitely Be a Buying Opportunity This Year** On March 26th, Yi Lihua, founder of Liquid Capital (formerly LD Capital), published an article stating that the war is unlikely to end in the short term, and the market is waiting for further clarification of the war. However, there will definitely be a great buying opportunity this year, and it is advisable to prepare as much ammunition as possible before then. Leaving aside the crypto industry, I've recently been studying AI and have been deeply impressed, especially by the efficiency of directing AI. This isn't just a simple efficiency improvement; it's like going directly from horse-drawn carriages to the airplane era. I require all the companies I invest in to immediately learn to use and embrace AI. In the future, companies that don't use AI will be eliminated. Today, thanks to AI, all companies and individuals are basically on the same starting line. Another major wave is coming, and my goal for myself this year is to spend half my time working collaboratively with AI.

Important Economic Developments

▌European Parliament Approves EU-US Trade Agreement

On March 26, the European Parliament voted on the EU-US trade agreement, which was ultimately approved. On March 19, the European Parliament's International Trade Committee, with 29 votes in favor, resumed the legislative approval process for the EU-US trade agreement. The EU and the US are expected to reach a trade agreement in July 2025.

Golden Encyclopedia

▌Can Bitcoin Still Be Called "Digital Gold" During Geopolitical Crises?

Every geopolitical crisis causes gold prices to rise while Bitcoin prices plummet. After six tests, the claim of "digital gold" has never been supported by data. Countries hoard gold but exclude Bitcoin from their reserves. For investors, Bitcoin exhibits asymmetry: it falls with stocks but does not rise with them. Three structural asymmetries prevent Bitcoin from achieving safe-haven status: an oversupply of derivatives (market structure), the dominance of leveraged traders (participant composition), and a lack of repeatable behavioral records (behavioral accumulation).

Bitcoin is not a safe-haven asset, but it is a "crisis-useful asset," which can indeed function when borders are closed and banks fail. If these three asymmetries are reduced, Bitcoin may no longer be a replica of gold but rather a completely new "next-generation gold."

Generational shifts and the widespread application of algorithms are key factors that could accelerate this process.

data.title: Humans are not ready for AI shopping and payments.
data.description:
AI Shopping, Amazon Says No

Imagine this: You tell AI, "Find me a pair of running shoes under $120, suitable for flat feet, with the best reviews," and it automatically visits Amazon, Walmart, and Nike, compares prices, reads reviews, filters out merchant advertisements, and places the order directly. You don't need to tap the screen once during the entire process.

Isn't that wonderful? And incredibly cost-effective?

This isn't just a fantasy; it's already happening.

Perplexity launched a feature called "Comet" that works exactly like this. But Amazon quickly sued Perplexity.

On March 9, 2026, the U.S. District Court for the Western District of Washington issued a temporary injunction against Amazon, prohibiting Comet from accessing the Amazon website. A week later, the Ninth Circuit Court of Appeals suspended the injunction. Comet was reinstated, but the legal battle had only just begun. On the surface, it's a legal battle, but in reality, it's an advertising battle. This battle, ostensibly a technical dispute, is actually about a fundamental question: When a human authorizes an AI to spend money on their behalf, does the platform have the right to exclude the AI? Amazon's legal basis is the 1986 Computer Fraud and Abuse Act (CFAA). It accuses Perplexity of "intentionally bypassing" the rules by disguising Comet's identity as a legitimate Chrome browser user. This accusation has some merit. The CFAA was designed to combat hacking into computer systems, and one provision prohibits "access to a computer beyond authorized limits." Amazon's logic is that human users' authorization on Amazon is for real people; AI using a fake identity exceeds that authorization. Perplexity's rebuttal is direct: your real motive is to protect $56 billion in advertising revenue. A large portion of Amazon's profits comes from merchants purchasing "sponsored product" placements—a significant portion of the "recommendations" you see in search results are paid for by merchants. AI agents don't have eyes and aren't attracted by misleading banner ads; they only provide the truly optimal solution. For Amazon, this "eyeless" characteristic of AI directly shakes its advertising system. Interestingly, the court initially sided with Amazon. The federal district court's logic was that even if a human user authorized AI to shop on their behalf, the platform has the right to refuse service based on "non-human access." The implication is: platform authorization takes precedence over user authorization—if you want AI to enter, you must first ask the platform's permission. The Ninth Circuit Court's stay order temporarily shelved this judgment but didn't negate the logic, only stating that "the evidence is currently insufficient to uphold the injunction." The case will continue. But more noteworthy than the outcome of this lawsuit is the real problem it exposes: the entire payment system, from its inception, never considered AI as a payment provider. Current payment systems are designed for humans. Financial services, such as shopping websites, require users to undergo KYC (Know Your Customer) procedures, such as uploading passport scans, undergoing facial recognition, and sometimes even requiring printed documents for signing and a photo taken while holding the document. CAPTCHAs require you to recognize traffic lights and zebra crossings. Credit card settlements take T+1 to T+7 and incur fixed fees—the standard rate for US merchants is 2.9% plus $0.30 per transaction, with an additional 1% for international cards. This set of rules is very user-friendly for human consumers. However, for AI as the payer, all of this is completely irrelevant—its consumption logic is entirely different from that of humans. The transaction characteristics of AI agents are high frequency, extremely small amounts, and 24/7 operation. Each API call costs $0.0001, reading a single line of data costs $0.00001, while purchasing one second of GPU computing power might only cost $0.000001. With traditional credit card payments, transaction fees alone consume the entire transaction amount. This isn't a problem that can be solved by optimizing the user experience; the entire infrastructure may need to be changed. Therefore, in 2026, tech companies, financial institutions, and Web3 developers are rebuilding a payment architecture for AI. According to my understanding, this involves roughly three layers. The first layer: Identity. Previously called KYC (Know Your Customer). Now, it's called KYA (Know Your Agent). The KYA protocol needs to answer three questions: Who does this Agent represent? What are its single payment limits and validity periods? Does it have any history of defaults? Skyfire combines OAuth authorization with on-chain cryptographic credentials, issuing the AI ​​a digital passport as an "authorized agent." Merchants, seeing this passport, know that a real human user is responsible for the transaction, and can find the right person if there are any problems. F5's cloud defense system can identify legitimate AI traffic carrying KYA tokens, distinguishing it from malicious crawlers. Merchants are willing to open their checkout pages to this type of AI because they know it is "authenticated" and not there to steal data. There is also a technological foundation: TEE, Trusted Execution Environment. The role of TEE is to provide a hardware-level safe for the AI's private keys. The mainstream solution is Intel SGX or TDX, where the core logic of the AI ​​Agent runs in a physically isolated encrypted chamber. The direct purpose of this design is security—even if the cloud server is compromised, attackers cannot see the private key and cannot tamper with payment decisions. However, it solves more than just security issues. It transforms the legal concept of "authorization is not the same as transfer" into a verifiable technical fact: users grant AI the authority to spend money, but AI can only invoke signatures within preset rules; it cannot sign outside these rules. The second layer: Protocols. Machines should not simulate human clicks on web pages. The HTTP status code 402, defined in 1999, has never been used. Coinbase and Cloudflare activated it in late 2025. The workflow is simple: the AI ​​requests data from the server, the server says "This data costs 0.01 USDC", and returns a 402 status code and the receiving address; the AI ​​wallet automatically generates a cryptographic signature, attaches it to the request header and sends it out; the server verifies the signature, funds are settled in real time through the L2 network, and the data is returned directly. The whole process takes less than a second, with no redirects, no pop-ups, and no CAPTCHAs. Stripe and Visa's MPP protocol goes even further, supporting a "one-time authorization, multiple expenditures" session mode: users give the AI ​​a 24-hour valid shopping window, setting a $200 limit. All transactions within this range are automatically settled by the AI, without requiring human confirmation for each transaction. Stripe internally calls this "the money version of OAuth". The third layer: Settlement. At this level, stablecoins are the only answer. Why not bank transfers? Not PayPal? Not a points system? Because stablecoins operate on a blockchain, making them programmable. Bank transfers are batch settlements; money sent today might not arrive for two days, creating a window of opportunity, tying up funds, and posing counterparty risk. Stablecoins, on the other hand, have millisecond-level settlement times, eliminating window periods and ensuring real-time fund arrival. USDC on the Base chain has a single transaction fee of less than $0.0001, with settlement completed in 200 milliseconds. More importantly, it supports "streaming payments"—for every second of GPU computing power used by AI, the corresponding amount flows in real-time, perfectly billed based on usage. This is a fundamental change for AI commercial applications: AI service providers can charge based on actual consumption, without prepayment, locked funds, or concerns about price fluctuations during the settlement cycle. The AI's wallet isn't a human-style wallet with a mnemonic phrase; instead, it's a highly customized smart contract with three embedded security valves: Whitelist Mechanism: Funds can only flow to legitimate merchants verified by Skyfire KYA. Not a single penny can be transferred to unverified addresses. This eliminates the risk of the AI ​​being hijacked and transferring funds arbitrarily. Idempotency Protection: Prevents the agent from being charged repeatedly for the same order due to logical loops or network instability. Multiple charges for a single confirmation is a major problem in traditional payments, but it can be avoided through design in smart contracts. Circuit Breaker: This prevents the AI ​​from triggering abnormal transactions. If the Agent initiates more transactions than a preset threshold within a minute—for example, a maximum of 10 transactions per minute in a normal scenario, suddenly increasing to 1000—the contract automatically locks, all transactions are suspended, and humans are called in to investigate what happened. This is not an option, but a necessary security design.

Four Legal Issues to be Resolved

A well-designed technical solution does not automatically eliminate legal issues.

Who compensates if the AI ​​makes a mistake?

If the AI ​​misreads product information, treating the unit price as the total price and placing a $1 million order—who is liable?

Under current agency law, if the merchant has reason to believe that the AI ​​represents the user's true intentions, the contractual liability usually rests with the user. But this logic needs to be patched.

One possible solution is to force human confirmation for orders exceeding five times the user's average daily spending, even if the AI ​​has full authorization. This mechanism is called a "digital cooling-off period." Without this mechanism, such orders should be legally considered revocable. AI's Duty of Loyalty: If Perplexity's Agent deliberately avoids low-priced platforms with which it has no partnership, recommending higher-priced products for which it receives commissions—would this constitute a breach of contract? As the user's agent, AI should seek the optimal benefit under the constraints set by the user. If a service provider manipulates AI's choices through algorithms for personal gain without clearly informing the user, the user has the right to sue under the Consumer Rights Protection Law and agency law. This is not a theoretical risk. AI recommendation algorithms are inherently susceptible to contamination from "bid-for-performance" rankings. Without mandatory transparency requirements, users have no idea whether their agent is setting traps for them. How can AML penetrate AI transactions? AI's high-frequency, micro-amount payments are a natural breeding ground for money laundering—each transaction is too small and too frequent for traditional risk control models to monitor. The solution lies at the protocol layer. The x402 "Facilitator" role must fulfill KYT responsibilities, monitoring in real time whether on-chain funds are flowing to sanctioned addresses. Every stablecoin change must be traceable to the actual "carbon-based beneficiary." Once an AI system is used for illegal money laundering, not only will the company face regulatory penalties, but relevant personnel may also be held personally liable. The idea of ​​"human-machine shared responsibility" simply means that the coders cannot hide behind the company to escape responsibility. Algorithm engineers who know this code will be used for money laundering and still deliver it are no different from directly participating in a crime. This logic is not without basis in the current legal framework—the provisions on joint crimes in the Criminal Law can precisely cover this situation of "knowingly committing a crime." Who keeps your wallet safe? For AI to help humans spend money, it must hold the user's payment credentials—private keys, platform login permissions, and even biometric tokens. This scenario is progressing faster than most people imagine. Today, AI can help users buy plane tickets, book hotels, and buy running shoes; the next step is paying utilities, credit card bills, and managing daily expenses. AI holds the user's bank account password, payment password, and login permissions for various platforms. Here's a question most people haven't considered: handing over credentials to AI and handing over your rights to AI are two different things. To illustrate this with a real-life example: Giving your bank card to your children for emergency use doesn't mean you're authorizing them to freely manage every penny in your account. There are implicit boundaries, and both parties are aware of them, even if they're not explicitly written down. The same principle applies to AI holding users' payment credentials—authorizing it to spend money on your behalf doesn't mean authorizing it to spend it however it wants. However, legally, this boundary is currently ambiguous. No precedent clarifies the scope within which AI has the right to spend money on your behalf, and what limits require your confirmation. Solving this problem requires simultaneous progress on two levels. On the legal level, simply including a "limited authorization" clause in the user agreement is insufficient; ultimately, precedent and legislation are needed to provide a comprehensive framework. Currently, the legal status, liability boundaries, and scope of authorization for AI agents are not systematically defined in any country. The ongoing lawsuit between Amazon and Perplexity will likely lead to US court rulings serving as one of the earliest benchmarks. Before that, each case had to be handled individually. The same applies to regulation—the existing financial regulatory framework was designed for financial institutions, not AI service providers, and this regulatory gap won't disappear on its own. On the technical level, protocols alone aren't enough; code is also needed to enforce this restriction. TEE hardware isolation is one solution, but a larger direction deserves attention: blockchain. The core characteristic of blockchain is decentralized storage—data isn't concentrated on a single server but distributed across multiple nodes, meaning that a breach of any single point won't lead to a large-scale leak. Applying this characteristic to AI payment scenarios, user payment credentials can be stored on the blockchain, not in the AI ​​service provider's database. When AI retrieves credentials, private key fragments participate in calculations on the blockchain but are never fully extracted and exposed in memory. This means that even the AI ​​service provider itself cannot unilaterally use user funds. In this sense, both the technical and legal levels are on the same path: both are addressing the question of "who is responsible." Law relies on accountability systems, and technology relies on architectural design; both ultimately point to the same goal—ensuring that every penny spent by AI has a traceable responsible party. What lies beyond the entry point? The wall between AI and shopping, AI and payments, will inevitably be torn down, not because of court rulings, but because the trend won't be reversed by a single service agreement. The payment revolution is just the first domino. When AI can spend money on behalf of humans, it gains a passport to the real world. The following scenarios are not hard to imagine: AI negotiating salaries for you, making investment decisions for you, participating in supply chain settlements, and purchasing digital assets in the virtual world. Once these things happen, the existing civil law system, financial regulatory framework, and data ownership concepts will all face pressure for restructuring. This is not alarmist. Each generation of fundamental internet transformations gives rise to a completely new set of legal issues. The PC era had computer crime, the internet era had privacy violations and platform liability, and the mobile internet era has mobile payments and fintech regulation—in each wave, lawyers are not bystanders, but rather among the first to rush in and establish the rules. The combination of Web3 and AI is the main theme of the next generation of the internet. And Mankiw's law firm focuses precisely on the legal issues unfolding in this new world: the compliance paths for stablecoins and crypto payments, the boundaries of responsibility for AI agents, the validity of smart contracts, and the regulatory jurisdiction of Web3 projects. These issues won't become meaningful until regulatory documents are issued—they are happening now.

data.title: Golden Web3.0 Daily Report | French President to Attend Bitcoin and Crypto Conference
data.description:
DeFi data

1. Total market value of DeFi tokens: 51.071 billion US dollars

DeFi Total Market Cap Data Source: coingecko

2. Trading volume of decentralized exchanges in the past 24 hours: $38.18

Trading volume of decentralized exchanges in the past 24 hours. Data source: coingecko

3. Assets locked in DeFi: $954.41 billion$100 million

Top 10 DeFi Projects by Locked Assets and Total Value Locked. Data Source: defillama

NFT Data

1. Total Market Value of NFTs: $7.508 billion

2.24 hours NFT transaction volume 1.22 billion US dollars

Headline

French President Macron to Attend Bitcoin and Crypto Conference

According to market news: French President Macron has confirmed that he will deliver a speech at the Bitcoin and Cryptocurrency Conference next month, becoming the first European head of state to attend a blockchain event.

NFT Total Market Cap, Top Ten Market Cap Projects Data Source: Coinmarketcap

Headline

French President Macron will attend Bitcoin and Crypto Conference

According to market news: French President Macron has confirmed that he will deliver a speech at the Bitcoin and Cryptocurrency Conference next month, becoming the first European head of state to attend a blockchain event.

...h2>

DeFi Hot Topics

1. MilkyWay Announces L1 Mainnet Shutdown, All Assets Returned to Native Chains

MilkyWay officially announced that it has formally initiated the L1 mainnet shutdown process. Before the shutdown, the team completed an on-chain upgrade and securely migrated all user assets from the MilkyWay L1 chain back to their respective native chains. This measure aims to ensure that user assets are completely and securely returned to their original blockchain networks. According to the announcement, users who previously held assets on MilkyWay should now be able to view their assets on their corresponding original networks. For example, TIA assets transferred from Celestia to MilkyWay should now be displayed again on the Celestia network. If users are unable to see their assets, the official team recommends submitting their wallet address to the official team or leaving a message under the announcement post for further assistance.

2. Ethereum privacy-focused EVM L2 network Payy raises $6 million, led by FirstMark Capital. On March 26th, Payy, an Ethereum privacy-focused EVM L2 network, raised $6 million, led by FirstMark Capital, with participation from Robot Ventures and DBA Crypto, among others. 3. Solana core development team Anza launches Constellation protocol, achieving a 50-millisecond block cycle. On March 26th, according to market news, Anza, the core development team of Solana, launched the Constellation protocol, a multi-concurrency proposer protocol that shortens Solana's block cycle to 50 milliseconds, making it the blockchain with the fastest economic takt time at the protocol level. This protocol replaces the single-leader model, eliminating exclusive control over transaction inclusion and ordering through concurrent proposers, enforcing fairness at the protocol level, without relying on trusted third parties or modifying clients, and keeping the validator economic model largely unchanged.

4. Solana Foundation Updates Validator Delegation Program Requirements, Effective May 1st

On March 26th, according to market news, the Solana Foundation announced that it will implement new validator delegation program requirements starting May 1st, 2026, focusing on transaction ordering fairness, prohibiting censorship, stricter timing rules, and limiting ASN and data center concentration. This adjustment aims to improve network performance and user experience, and the monitoring platform Ghost will assist in tracking and publishing network health metrics.

... 5. DeFi On-Chain Revenue to Reach $8 Billion in 2025, Over Half of Stablecoin Deposit Yields Lower Than US Treasury Bonds On March 26th, according to researcher Vadym's analysis, DeFi is projected to generate approximately $8 billion in on-chain revenue in 2025. The largest source is AMM transaction fees, approximately $4.2 billion, with Uniswap, Meteora, and Raydium accounting for 62%. Lending interest is the second largest contributor, approximately $1.76 billion, with money markets such as Aave and Morpho contributing over 60% of DeFi's total TVL, although about half of the lending demand is revolving leverage. RWA will contribute $600-900 million, with US Treasury bonds accounting for approximately 41% of the RWA market. Perpetual contract funding rates will contribute approximately $300 million, mainly from Ethena. Notably, over half of the stablecoin deposits in the Ethereum ecosystem yield less than US Treasury bond rates. Potential revenue streams such as insurance underwriting and on-chain options remain largely untapped. An analysis using Sky (formerly MakerDAO) as an example points out that approximately 70% of its revenue comes from off-chain assets, reflecting the accelerating flow of TradeFi returns into DeFi through permissioned channels. Disclaimer: Jinse Finance, as a blockchain information platform, provides articles for informational purposes only and does not constitute actual investment advice. Please establish sound investment principles and be sure to enhance your risk awareness.

data.title: The Hormuz is not under blockade, but the dollar is being bypassed.
data.description:
Several million dollars at a time, but not accepted in US dollars?

Is the RMB quietly gaining prominence?

The RMB quietly taking the lead?

De-dollarization is no longer just a narrative

Every time millions of dollars in tolls are paid in RMB, it not only represents that this part of the energy flow is bypassing the dollar settlement system, but also represents a series of resounding bargaining chips in the global "de-dollarization" process. Amidst a still-strong US dollar and rising yields due to Federal Reserve interest rate hikes, global central banks (especially China, Russia, and India) are quietly accumulating gold, awaiting signals of a "long-term de-dollarization trade." This new regulation in the Strait of Hormuz directly provides the RMB with a crucial, high-value, and mandatory international use scenario. Shipping companies that need to pass through the strait, whether they like it or not, need to "absorb" RMB to meet the IRGC's payment requirements. This means that the actual circulation and acceptance of RMB in international trade is being enhanced in a non-market-based way. Furthermore, there is an even more noteworthy detail. Reports indicate that Iran has allowed crude oil tankers settled in RMB to pass. It's important to understand that in traditional energy trade, the settlement path is almost fixed: pricing is in US dollars, clearing is done through the banking system, and ultimately enters a financial cycle centered on the US dollar. However, in the current environment, some transactions are beginning to "shift"—they can be settled in RMB. The changes brought about by this are not just "more payment methods," but rather: Energy trading is beginning to have the ability to "operate independently of the dollar path." And once this ability exists, it will create a chain reaction: whoever can be accepted as a means of payment will be able to enter this trading system. In this sense, the RMB is more like passively entering the circulation process in a specific scenario. In other words, a passive "currency penetration" is unfolding. The insurance industry's "divine assistance" has provided unexpected structural support for the IRGC's "new rules." The international insurance market, represented by Lloyd’s of London, has begun to reassess the region’s risk structure. Market feedback shows: Vessels without "passage confirmation" → extremely high risk → difficult to insure. Vessels with confirmation → significantly reduced risk → insurable. This means that whether or not one is "allowed to pass" begins to influence financial pricing. And insurance, in essence, is the "underlying credit switch" for global trade. Once the insurance system is adjusted, the following will occur: Unqualified vessels, even if physically able to pass inspection, will be unable to complete commercial transactions; qualified vessels, on the other hand, will find it easier to obtain financial support. In other words, unexpectedly for governments, the international insurance market, through its actuarial models, indirectly acknowledged the IRGC's de facto control over the Strait, making IRGC's permission a prerequisite for the smooth operation of commercial shipping, thus completing a structural "legitimization" process of financial power. The Strait of Hormuz is not closed. Oil is still flowing.

But the change has occurred at a deeper level:

  • Who can pass  

  • What currency is used for settlement?

  • Who defines risk?

As the answers to these core questions begin to diverge, a longer-term trend may be forming: not a global collective abandonment of the dollar, but ratheran increasing number of key trade and financial transactions no longer necessarily require the use of the dollar. The Strait of Hormuz, a strategic waterway carrying 30% of the world's maritime oil trade, is accelerating a global financial and geopolitical transformation in an unprecedented way.

data.title: Bitrefill security incident analysis and trace of stolen funds.
data.description:
On March 17, Bitrefill officially disclosed a cyberattack that occurred on March 1. The attack method had many similarities to previous cyberattacks by Lazarus Group / BlueNoroff against other companies in the crypto asset industry. The Beosin security team, combining collected threat intelligence and information publicly available from Bitrefill, conducted an attack methodology and fund tracking analysis of this incident, and shares the results as follows: Attack Method Analysis According to Bitrefill, the attack initially involved hacking into an employee's laptop and stealing old credentials. Wallet Access Permissions: 18,500 order records were exported in bulk, including user emails, encrypted addresses, and IP addresses; and the purchase and consumption of gift card inventory were forged.

https://x.com/bitrefill/status/2033931580352221656

Stolen Funds Tracking

Combining threat intelligence and on-chain transaction data,

Analysis of Stolen Funds Flow by Beosin Trace

Among them, address 0x5a0128e21cb8dc515ab8c4e5079b1f0444e92763 transferred 174 ETH into Tornado Cash. Addressing the difficulty of tracing funds in coin mixing protocols like Tornado Cash, Beosin, drawing on its experience in tracing multiple coin mixing money laundering cases, continuously monitored all deposit and withdrawal data. Through multi-dimensional correlation analysis of transaction timing, amount characteristics, and behavioral patterns, and using its self-developed intelligent tracking algorithm, it achieved penetration of the coin mixing fund chain and identified the withdrawal address: 0x3d79f9012a13fe7948daaee3b8e9118371450d69. The address then performed a cross-chain exchange from ETH to RON, converting 1.79 ETH into 1.79 ETH. text="">413,763.75 USDT. Currently this addressTVfA8wz2quUvRvhqs8VtnCeMyV2VzFAW9Rshares575,212.91 USDTPrecipitation. The above addresses have all been marked as high-risk addresses by Beosin KYT. For example, the address shown is: [Image of Beosin KYT address].

data.title: The $2 that disappeared in "shadow hosting": an AI overreach incident
data.description:
The illusion of artificial intelligence was once considered a harmless information bias—fabricating a fact or presenting a illogical text. But the reality of 2026 delivered a harsh blow: as AI evolves from chatbots into agents, the illusion begins to transform into a costly execution risk. The problem is no longer simply about saying the wrong thing; it now has the ability to directly take away your assets. Supply Chain Poison: From Hand-Written Code to Vibe Coding This week's LiteLLM poisoning incident serves as a wake-up call for this trend. As the underlying engine of almost all mainstream AI Agent frameworks, LiteLLM suffered a typical supply chain breach in this incident. Attackers exploited weaknesses in the security toolchain to obtain the distribution key, thereby pushing malicious code to user environments as an official version. Due to the presence of a legitimate signature, traditional verification mechanisms were almost rendered ineffective. Interestingly, this heist was exposed simply because the hacker's code contained a basic bug in its handling of recursive logic, causing the victim's computer to crash due to resource exhaustion. This exposes a long-neglected vulnerability in the open-source ecosystem: when you install a library, you are essentially trusting an entire dependency tree spanning hundreds of packages. The decay of any node in this tree will flow down the chain into the core production environment. This risk is further amplified under the popular Vibe coding development paradigm. Many developers describe their requirements using natural language, with AI automatically generating code. When system errors occur, developers often directly adopt the AI's suggested fixes and perform operations such as `pip install`, lacking further verification of the dependency sources and security. In this environment, the development threshold continues to decrease, but the complexity of security audits has not decreased accordingly. Every seemingly simple dependency installation actually introduces new uncertainties, and these uncertainties are gradually becoming part of the systemic risk. Meta Internal Incident: When Humans Become Execution Interfaces Similar changes are also occurring at the human-computer interaction level. As software development shifts from step-by-step operations to result-driven approaches, the role of humans is changing: from judges to confirmation nodes, or even just execution interfaces. Software development is undergoing a qualitative leap from "manual transmission" to "autonomous driving." In the past, developers were responsible for every operation; but with the introduction of agents, humans are more like passengers in an autonomous driving system, gradually retreating to confirmation nodes in the process, or even just execution terminals. When the human role degenerates from "judgmenter" to AI's "execution interface," the willingness to review decreases exponentially. A recent SEV1 security incident at Meta demonstrates this: an engineer invoked an AI agent to answer technical questions on an internal forum, and the agent automatically posted replies without review. Subsequently, another engineer followed the suggestion, but inaccurate information led to misconfigured system permissions, leaving sensitive data exposed without authorization for two hours. Meta officially attributed this to "human error," but from a human-computer interaction perspective, it appears more like an interface crash. When AI's output appears so professional and "executable," the defense mechanisms of humans as verification nodes automatically weaken. This change can be mitigated in information systems through rollbacks and repairs, but when AI begins to intervene in systems with real-world consequences—especially in financial scenarios involving assets and transactions—the illusion becomes an irreversible and expensive bill. The $2 Lesson: A Self-Initiated Rescue Through Substitution If Meta's incident was a misallocation of permissions, then in the financial sector, the problem is much more serious because it directly concerns asset ownership. Since 2026, numerous wallet and infrastructure projects have launched Agentic Wallet products, attempting to enable AI agents to directly assist users in completing on-chain operations. When conducting systematic testing on this emerging product category, the Cobo AI team captured a highly representative behavioral pattern, which they defined as "Shadow Custody"—that is, an agent, without the user's knowledge, transfers actual control of assets from the user's wallet to an invisible and uncontrollable intermediary by autonomously generating keys and creating temporary addresses. The result is an address on the blockchain that the user cannot directly control—a black box that exists logically but is invisible to the user. The sequence of events can be summarized as follows: A user instructed an agent to purchase $2 worth of "Spain YES" tokens on Polymarket. During execution, the agent quickly encountered an obstacle: Polymarket transactions require a specific digital signature format (EIP-712), but the SDK used by the agent was designed to bundle the "assembling signature content" and "signing with the private key" steps together—in other words, the SDK assumed you already had a private key and could sign it yourself. The problem is that the user's wallet isn't a typical wallet where the user holds the key; it's an MPC wallet where the keys are jointly held by multiple parties—it's perfectly capable of signing, just using a different path. However, the Agent isn't aware of this path; it only sees that the current path is blocked and concludes: This wallet cannot sign. In a system where trust is maintained by rules, the process should stop or request additional authorization. But the Agent doesn't stop. Instead, it interprets this limitation as the existing wallet being unable to complete the signing and then searches for alternative solutions. So, without authorization, the Agent generated a brand new private key locally and used it to create a temporary wallet address. It then transferred 2 USDC.e tokens from the user's MPC wallet to this address and used the temporary key to complete the signing, successfully purchasing the tokens. From the transaction results, everything happened as expected: 10 "Spain YES" conditional tokens were correctly purchased. However, from a system structure perspective, it completely messed things up. These tokens did not return to the user's MPC wallet but remained in the temporary address created by the Agent. The user initially didn't notice until checking the balance and finding it to be zero, at which point the Agent reconstructed the entire execution process. This wasn't just a bug; logically speaking, the Agent didn't make a mistake; it merely filled a gap in the system's boundary definition. Simply put, when the original path was blocked, the Agent didn't stop and report an error; instead, it took a detour to "complete the task." It secretly created a temporary address to transfer funds, yet the UI didn't mention that the assets in its hands had already been "moved." This disconnect between user perception and underlying reality is essentially a form of path hijacking. This covert path hijacking reflects the systemic risks inherent in machine-native finance regarding the transparency of fund flows, semantic consistency, and the stability of the execution environment: And this "filling"—once unrestrained—opens three doors for attackers: Logical Deflection: Malicious tools don't need to forge links or pop-ups; they only need to trick the Agent into activating a temporary wallet deep within the code. The user sees a successful transaction, but in reality, control of the assets is stripped away the instant the transaction is executed. Semantic Manipulation: Attackers don't need to infiltrate the system; they only need to implant fake technical restrictions (such as "original path has expired") in documents or prompts. The task-oriented Agent will actively transfer funds to circumvent the "fake restriction." It wasn't hacked, it was just tricked.

  • Component poisoning: By tampering with third-party SDKs or API libraries, correct road signs are made to appear as dead ends. The agent may even believe it is faithfully taking a detour, when in fact it has entered a well-designed dead end.

    • Constraints are more important than capabilities: Three gates to controlling the boundaries of AI agent behavior

    In financial scenarios with zero tolerance for error, the "intelligence" of AI is often the biggest enemy of security. What we need is not stronger execution, but a more rigid ability to define boundaries.

    In financial scenarios with zero tolerance for error, the "intelligence" of AI is often the biggest enemy of security. What we need is not stronger execution, but stronger ability to define boundaries.

    To this end, we need to install three insurmountable gates for the Agent: 1. Policy Gate: Breaking the self-reference dilemma. Constraints must come from the outside. We need a separate Policy Engine to determine "whether the operation is allowed" before the action occurs. For example, Agents are strictly prohibited from generating their own private keys or transferring funds to unauthorized addresses. Once this line is crossed, execution terminates immediately. 2. Transaction Gate: Risk Perspective All Agent decisions must ultimately be translated into on-chain transactions. A "transaction firewall" is established between the Agent and the blockchain to restore unreadable raw data to structured information. By scoring the target address's behavior, amount deviation, and other dimensions, high-risk transactions will be blocked and forced to trigger manual approval. 3. Visibility Gate: Real-time Observation Introducing an agent-independent watcher system. When funds flow to a new address or fail to return within a specified time, the watcher sends alerts within seconds. It transforms post-event discovery into process intervention, ensuring that asset status remains transparent even if the agent attempts to conceal the path. Conclusion We are in a dangerous transition period. AI's execution capabilities are skyrocketing, but our constraints on it are still stuck in the Stone Age. If an architecture cannot clearly define absolutely prohibited behaviors and perform real-time verification, then every autonomous decision made by AI is essentially a high-stakes gamble with users' assets. And in the financial sector, nobody wants to gamble.

data.title: Why is Wall Street going blockchain?
data.description: Wall Street is migrating its capital markets infrastructure to the blockchain to improve the speed of money transfers and asset tokenization, eliminating hidden costs in the existing system. Major institutions such as the DTCC and the New York Stock Exchange have begun this move, signaling the largest infrastructure upgrade in financial markets since electronic trading, creating tremendous opportunities for building on-chain financial services. Wall Street is no longer just exploring blockchain. It is migrating to blockchain. After years of observation, the institutions that form the backbone of global capital markets—exchanges, clearinghouses, and electronic trading platforms—are migrating to on-chain. What is happening now is the largest infrastructure upgrade in capital markets since the shift to electronic trading thirty years ago. But most people won't realize this until the transition is complete. Institutions have already begun to act. In December 2025, the DTCC received a no-action letter from the SEC authorizing it to tokenize real-world assets on approved blockchains. The DTCC processed $3.7 quadrillion in transactions in 2024. It now plans to launch production-grade tokenization services for U.S. Treasury securities in the first half of 2026. On January 19, 2026, the New York Stock Exchange announced a platform for 24/7 on-chain trading and settlement of U.S. stocks and ETFs—including fragmented shares, instant settlement, and stablecoin funding—and partnered with BNY and Citibank to support tokenized deposits at the ICE Clearing House. The world's most iconic stock exchange is moving on-chain. Tradeweb completed its first real-time, fully on-chain U.S. Treasury financing for USDC in August 2025—on a Saturday, outside the traditional settlement window, in collaboration with Bank of America, Citadel Securities, DTCC, and Virtu Financial. Its scope is expanding quarterly and now includes cross-border and intraday settlement. Nasdaq submitted its proposed rule changes to the SEC in September 2025. This increasingly looks like a migration rather than a series of isolated experiments. Regulatory Clarity and Opportunities for Builders The ultimate breakthrough is regulatory clarity—and it's finally in action. If the current momentum continues, the CLARITY Act can provide traditional finance with what the Genius Act has already done for the adoption and acceleration of stablecoins. The safeguards that large institutions need are within reach. So what does this mean for builders? The migration of the world's financial infrastructure to on-chain will create demand for entirely new categories of products and services. The fastest-moving existing companies aren't your competitors—they're your customers. DTCC doesn't want to build middleware. The NYSE doesn't want to build compliance tools. Tradeweb doesn't want to build a cross-border distribution layer. These companies are laying the foundation for regulated, institutional-grade infrastructure. The founders are building everything that runs on top of it. This is the same as in the 1990s. Exchanges didn't build E*TRADE. They didn't build Bloomberg. They didn't build the order management systems and prime broker platforms that defined the next generation. Those were built by founders who saw the trends of the future. More participants, faster speeds, lower friction. More liquidity. A larger market. History clearly shows where this is going. The window of opportunity to build infrastructure in tokenized financial markets is now open. Build accordingly.

data.title: Why Did a U.S. Court Dismiss Michael Lewellen’s Bid to Shield His Crypto Software From Prosecution?
data.description:
Software Developers Caught In Legal Limbo After Texas Court Ruling

A developer’s attempt to secure a legal "shield" for his code before it even launches has hit a wall in the United States.

Michael Lewellen, the creator behind a non-custodial crowdfunding tool called Pharos, recently asked a federal court to confirm that his software wouldn’t land him in prison.

He argued that because Pharos is designed to be private and non-custodial, meaning he never actually touches the users' money, he cannot possibly comply with federal money-laundering rules that require tracking user identities.

Essentially, he was asking for permission to innovate without the fear of a five-year prison sentence hanging over his head

Today, I’m taking a stand against the Biden administration’s unjust crackdown on crypto development. I’ve filed a lawsuit against the DOJ to challenge their flawed and unjust interpretation of the law.

My work on Pharos—a non-custodial protocol for public goods…

— Michael Lewellen (@LewellenMichael) January 16, 2025

On 25 March 2026, Chief Judge Reed O’Connor of the Northern District of Texas dismissed the case.

The court’s reasoning was simple: Lewellen isn't in trouble yet.

The judge ruled that the developer failed to show a "substantial threat of prosecution," effectively telling him that the court cannot solve a problem that hasn't happened.

While the dismissal was "without prejudice", meaning Lewellen can return if the government actually comes after him, the decision leaves a cloud of uncertainty over the decentralized finance (DeFi) sector.

🚨 JUST IN: Texas Court Dismisses Lawsuit against Pam Bondi over Non-Custodial Software Prosecutions

A Texas Court has dismissed a lawsuit by cryptocurrency developer Michael Lewellen filed against Attorney General Pam Bondi, in which he alleged that criminal cases brought… pic.twitter.com/lgxaZeIfN2

— The Rage (@theragetech) March 25, 2026

Does A Department Of Justice Memo Provide Enough Protection?

Central to the court’s decision was a document known as the "Blanche Memo," issued by Deputy Attorney General Todd Blanche in April 2025.

This internal Department of Justice (DOJ) guideline suggests that prosecutors should not target developers of "offline wallets" or mixing services for the independent actions of their users or for accidental regulatory slips.

Judge O’Connor pointed to this as a sign that Lewellen wasn't in immediate danger.

Lewellen, however, remains unconvinced that a shift in internal policy is the same as a legal guarantee.

Following the ruling, he expressed his frustration on social media.

Lewellen wrote,

"Disappointed to see the court dismiss my suit today. A non-binding DoJ memo is no substitute for real legal certainty."

Disappointed to see the court dismiss my suit today. A non-binding DoJ memo is no substitute for real legal certainty.

My lawyers are exploring all options for a path forward. Huge thanks to the @coincenter team for their incredible support and expertise through this.

We need… https://t.co/uXJqGww7IO

— Michael Lewellen (@LewellenMichael) March 25, 2026

His sentiment highlights a growing rift between the tech community and the judiciary over whether "regulation by memo" is a fair way to govern a multi-billion-dollar industry.

Why Are Tornado Cash And Samourai Wallet Cases Different?

To justify his fear of arrest, Lewellen pointed to the high-profile crackdowns on other crypto privacy tools.

Roman Storm, the co-founder of Tornado Cash, was convicted in 2025 of conspiracy to operate an unlicensed money-transmitting business, while Samourai Wallet founders Keonne Rodriguez and William Lonergan Hill are currently serving five-year sentences.

DOJ has decided it will retry Roman Storm in the fall. Despite failing to convince a jury the first time around, despite making obvious mistakes like calling irrelevant witnesses and not understanding the forensic analysis of their own blockchain evidence, and despite multiple… pic.twitter.com/MRZDHAugT8

— Amanda Tuminelli (@amandatums) March 10, 2026

Lewellen argued these cases prove that writing neutral code is now a criminal risk in the United States.

Judge O’Connor disagreed with the comparison.

He noted that in those cases, prosecutors alleged the developers knowingly allowed criminals to use their platforms to hide stolen funds.

O'Connor wrote,

"The ‘core conduct’ of those cases is money laundering. By contrast, the core conduct here would be running a business."

The judge emphasized that because Lewellen "disclaims any knowing transmission of criminal funds," his situation doesn't match the criminal profile of the developers currently behind bars.

Will Congress Step In To Define The Rules For DeFi?

With the courts refusing to weigh in early, the focus has shifted to the halls of Congress.

Industry advocates, including groups like Coin Center and the Blockchain Association, are now putting all their weight behind the Blockchain Regulatory Certainty Act (BRCA).

This bipartisan bill aims to explicitly exempt developers who do not control user funds from being classified as money transmitters.

Today, @RepRitchie and I introduced the Blockchain Regulatory Certainty Act to protect blockchain developers and service providers that never custody consumer funds from unjust government prosecution. pic.twitter.com/nCbpHcpf1Y

— Tom Emmer (@GOPMajorityWhip) May 21, 2025

Peter Van Valkenburgh, executive director of Coin Center, warned that relying on the government's good graces is a dangerous game for developers.

"The Blanche memo is not enough to secure their rights.”

He described it as a "vague enforcement signal" rather than a real limit on state power.

But that is not real clarity. A DOJ memo is not binding law. It can be revised, revoked, or ignored. And it plainly has not provided meaningful protection to developers, given the outcomes in the Tornado Cash and Samourai Wallet cases. 3/

— Peter Van Valkenburgh (@valkenburgh) March 25, 2026

So while I hope the court is right that non-custodial software developers are not at real risk, the Blanche memo is not enough to secure their rights. It is a vague enforcement signal, not a durable limit on government power. 4/ pic.twitter.com/XgzfQ8Astr

— Peter Van Valkenburgh (@valkenburgh) March 25, 2026

As the 2026 election season approaches, the window for passing such legislation is narrowing, leaving developers like Lewellen to wonder if they should keep building or wait for the law to catch up.

data.title: The AI ​​era is creating a polarization: the rich are getting richer and the poor are getting poorer.
data.description:
Author: jiayi Source: X, @mscryptojiayi

AI has changed our lifestyles; that's a fact.

We use AI to write emails, create PPTs, search for information, and even write our WeChat Moments posts. We've become accustomed to AI's presence, as natural as we've become accustomed to WiFi.

But few people stop to think about this question: Is the AI ​​you use the same thing as the AI ​​others use?

The "fairness" of the AI ​​era is the biggest illusion

Silicon Valley likes to tell a story: AI gives everyone a super assistant, knowledge is no longer the privilege of a few, and everyone is equal.

It sounds beautiful.

But the truth is—AI is inherently unfair; it's a competition of financial resources. From chips to computing power, from model training to token consumption, every aspect of AI is expensive. An NVIDIA H100 chip costs over $25,000. Training a GPT-4 level model costs over $100 million. Every question you ask AI consumes tokens—and tokens have a price. Claude Opus costs $5 per million tokens input and $25 per million tokens output. ChatGPT Pro costs $200 per month. Add to that Perplexity, Cursor, Midjourney… and a heavy AI user's monthly tool costs easily exceed $500. Some people are burning through $5,000 a month to build competitive barriers with AI, while others think they've caught up with the times using a free version of ChatGPT. These are not the same track. They're not even the same game. At the national level: the structural gap is irreversible. This logic is even more brutal at the national level. The AI ​​arms race requires three things: chips, computing power, and talent. All three require huge amounts of capital. The United States alone controls more than 70% of the world's AI computing power. China is catching up, but the chip ban is holding it back. As for most developing countries—in 46 emerging market countries, entry-level broadband costs account for 40% of monthly income. When even stable internet access is a luxury for a young Nigerian, what's the point of talking about "AI equality"? In high-income countries, 94% of people have internet access, compared to only 23% in low-income countries. 84% of high-income countries have 5G coverage, compared to only 4% in low-income countries. Third-world countries aren't just lagging behind in the AI ​​era; they're simply not even qualified to participate. This structural gap cannot be bridged through hard work alone. At the individual level: Your ceiling is being redefined by AI. The logic at the national level applies equally to individuals. As I wrote on my Twitter bio: An individual's ceiling = values ​​+ cognition + practical ability. What has AI done in these three ways? First, AI has solved a large number of practical efficiency problems. Previously, creating an industry report took a week; now it can be done in a day. Previously, writing code started from scratch; now AI provides the framework. In terms of efficiency, AI is indeed leveling the playing field. Second, however, AI is significantly amplifying cognitive gaps. With the same AI tool, what you ask, how you ask it, and whether you can judge whether the AI's answer is right or wrong—all depend entirely on your existing level of knowledge. A person with deep knowledge using Claude for research knows what questions to ask, how to follow up, and which answers have flaws and need verification. AI saved him 80% of his execution time, which he used for deeper thinking. But what about someone with shallow understanding? They throw questions at AI and use whatever AI gives them. They throw their brains at it and deliver directly. Over time, they stop thinking. AI didn't make them smarter; it made them lazy and stupid. Third, the gap in delivery quality will widen. When you ask AI questions based on your existing knowledge, the difference in depth, accuracy, and real-time performance is exponential. Using Claude Opus, one person produces deep insights, while another produces seemingly plausible but ultimately meaningless information. A particularly interesting study from Aalto University in Finland shows that the more people use AI, the more likely they are to overestimate their own abilities. AI makes you "feel" stronger—your output looks professional and fluent. But if you can't distinguish between good and bad, you're just producing "refined mediocrity." Therefore, the gaps in values, cognition, and practical ability—these three dimensions—are magnified infinitely in the AI ​​era. Smart people become smarter, those with knowledge have deeper knowledge, and the wealthy use better tools to widen the gap further. Meanwhile, those at the other end become lazier, more superficial, and poorer with the "help" of AI. Cost × Cognition: A Double Gap Exacerbated Here's a logical chain that many haven't grasped: Money determines the level of AI you can use → The level of AI determines the quality and depth of information you acquire → The quality of information determines your cognitive boundaries → Cognitive boundaries determine the quality of your decisions → The quality of decisions determines how much money you can earn. This is a closed loop. The rich will get richer, and the poor will get poorer. The free version of ChatGPT has a near 40% illusion rate. That means if you ask it 10 questions, 4 of the answers are fabricated. The paid version of GPT-4 has a 28% illusion rate, and the latest version has dropped to 45%. Decisions made with the free version and decisions made with Opus, accumulated over the long term, will result in two completely different life trajectories. A huge information gap will always exist in this world. AI hasn't eliminated the information gap; AI has turned it into a paywall. Those who use VPNs and those who don't live in two different worlds. Here's a personal observation that I find quite disheartening. You're likely seeing this article because you can access Twitter using a VPN. But think about it—how many people around you can't? When you talk to them, don't you clearly feel a gap in their understanding? This isn't a difference in intelligence. It's a long-term cognitive divide caused by the information environment. One person is exposed to the world's most cutting-edge information, in-depth discussions, and top-quality content creators daily. The other sees algorithm-fed short videos and filtered information feeds. Over five or ten years, their ways of thinking, judgment, and worldviews will be completely different. The AI ​​era has amplified this gap even further. Those who can access VPNs use Claude, Perplexity, and the world's best AI tools. For those who can't bypass the Great Firewall—ChatGPT is blocked in China, Claude is blocked in China—they can only use localized alternatives or obtain them through resellers at inflated prices. The "walls" of the AI ​​era aren't just physical firewalls. There are language walls—cutting-edge AI models are far more optimized for English than other languages. There are paywalls. There are algorithmic cocoons. Each wall divides people into different worlds. Stanford University research shows that non-English speaking users require five times more tokens to access the same content using AI. In other words, you spend the same amount of money but get less information of lower quality. The scariest thing: you've already fallen behind, but you don't know it. This is the point I most want to make in this entire article. Free AI can also answer questions. It can also help you write. It can also help you search. So people using the free version might think, "I'm using AI too, I'm not falling behind." But the free version's reasoning is shallower, its illusions more numerous, and its information older. The answers you get "seem" correct, but are actually full of seemingly plausible errors. It's like two people "running." One is actually running forward, the other is running in place on a treadmill. Both feel they are running, but only one is moving forward. There's a concept in psychology called the Dunning-Kruger effect: the less you know, the more you think you know. AI amplifies this effect tenfold—the more you rely on AI, the more powerful you feel. But you've lost the ability to think independently; you simply don't know. This is the cruelest aspect of the AI ​​era. It's not that AI will replace you. It's that people using better AI and with deeper understanding will leave you far behind. And you might not even understand how you fell behind until the day you're eliminated.
